extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) primarily employ **decentralized or ad-hoc architectures** rather than centralized frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** and **Air Quality Management** exemplars utilize **single-board computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi)** for localized data acquisition and processing, suggesting **multiple, distributed instances** of digital twins operating independently. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar explicitly mentions **AutomationML-based digital twins**, which may imply **modular, hierarchical structuring** for simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization services, but no explicit coordination mechanisms (e.g., federated twins or hierarchical synchronization) are described. The **gantry crane system** (implied by the C4 Model context) likely follows a **single-instance, containerized approach** with no mention of multiplicities or decentralization. **No explicit synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity** are specified in the documents for any exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin systems described adopt a **lightweight, ad-hoc orchestration approach** without relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **virtual machines (VMs) with pre-recorded data**, an **InfluxDB database**, and an **ActiveMQ message broker** for **event-driven communication**, indicating a **loosely coupled, service-oriented constellation**. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, integrating **AutomationML-based digital twins** with **Raspberry Pi sensor data**, suggesting a **hybrid cloud-edge architecture** with **modular service integration** (runtime visualization, simulation, prediction). The **gantry crane system** (implied by the C4 Model) likely follows a **containerized microservices pattern**, though no explicit orchestration framework (e.g., Kubernetes, Apache Kafka) is mentioned. **Resource management** is implied via **virtualization/containerization** (e.g., VMs, Raspberry Pi), but no dynamic scaling or auto-adaptation mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin exemplars demonstrate **limited horizontal integration** with external systems, relying on **ad-hoc or domain-specific protocols**. The **GreenhouseDT** uses **InfluxDB (time-series database)** and **ActiveMQ (message broker)** for internal data exchange but does not specify interoperability with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates **AutomationML** (a **construction-domain standard**) for digital twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** for cloud-based pipeline execution, but no explicit **APIs, OPC UA, or MQTT-based integration** with external systems (e.g., ERP, SCADA) is mentioned. The **gantry crane system** (implied by the C4 Model) lacks details on external dependencies, though its **cyber-physical nature** suggests potential integration with **logistics or warehouse management systems**, which is not elaborated. **No standardized interoperability frameworks** (e.g., FIWARE, OMG DDS) are referenced.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs **behavioral adaptation models** for both the physical system (e.g., greenhouse, air quality management) and its digital twin counterpart. In **GreenhouseDT**, the system leverages **prerecorded data** embedded in virtual machine images for offline simulation and behavioral analysis. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes **AutomationML-based digital twin models** for runtime visualization, simulation, and predictive analytics, with a focus on **model-driven engineering** for indoor air quality management. Data is sourced from **Raspberry Pi-based sensor networks** and processed via **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline execution. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar integrates an **InfluxDB database** for time-series data storage and an **ActiveMQ message broker** for real-time data exchange between the physical and digital systems. Model relationships are implicit, with the digital twin dynamically adapting to real-world conditions via **self-adaptive mechanisms** (e.g., behavioral adjustments in response to environmental changes).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar features a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time environmental monitoring, including parameters relevant to indoor air quality (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter, humidity, temperature). The **GreenhouseDT** system relies on **embedded sensors** (unspecified types) within the physical greenhouse, with data transmitted to the digital twin via **ActiveMQ** for processing. Spatial distribution and sampling frequencies are not explicitly detailed, but the use of **virtual machines with prerecorded data** in **GreenhouseDT** suggests offline or batch-based sensing. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar’s sensors are likely **high-precision** (given the context of virus spread prevention) but lack specific accuracy/precision metrics. Data collection mechanisms involve **direct sensor-to-Raspberry Pi transmission** and subsequent cloud-based processing via **Microsoft Azure**.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar emphasizes **model-driven digital twin engineering**, implying high fidelity in **simulation and predictive models** for indoor air quality dynamics. Validation methods are not explicitly stated, but the use of **AutomationML** suggests adherence to standardized engineering practices for model verification. **GreenhouseDT** focuses on **self-adaptive behavioral models**, where fidelity is maintained through **real-time synchronization** between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, enabled by **ActiveMQ-based messaging** and **InfluxDB time-series data**. Uncertainty sources (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) are not quantified, but the system’s reliance on **prerecorded data** in virtual machines may introduce offline validation challenges. Error detection/correction mechanisms are implicit in the **self-adaptive feedback loops** of **GreenhouseDT**, while the **Air Quality Management** exemplar’s **Azure-based pipeline** likely includes automated quality assurance for data consistency. No explicit error quantification or correction protocols are documented.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system involves real-time data transmission from the **Physical Crane** to the **Digital Twin** components via the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program). Key details include:
  - **Data Types Transmitted**: State data of the crane (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration, controller inputs, and operational status) are logged continuously.
  - **Communication Protocol**: Implicitly inferred as a **serial/TCP-based protocol** (given the Python interface connecting to hardware controllers) or potentially **MQTT** (since the Digital Twin uses an MQTT Broker for inter-service communication).
  - **Transmission Frequency**: Continuous logging is performed by the **Logging Application**, suggesting high-frequency (likely **sub-second or millisecond-level**) updates to the **TimescaleDB** database.
  - **Event Triggers**: Data is logged asynchronously in real-time, with no explicit event-based triggers mentioned beyond the crane's operational state changes.
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: No explicit mechanisms (e.g., checksums, validation rules) are described, but the **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories with simulations, implying post-hoc validation of logged data.
  - **Preprocessing Steps**: The **Logging Application** likely normalizes and formats raw sensor/controller data before storage in the **TimescaleDB** database.
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Data is centralized in the **TimescaleDB** database, which serves as the single source of truth for the Digital Twin. The **MQTT Broker** facilitates inter-service communication, ensuring synchronized data access across components like the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service**.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this system is mediated by the **Control Application** and **Physical Crane Interface**, which translate digital commands into physical actions. Key details include:
  - **Command Types**: Movement trajectories (generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service**) and operational control signals (e.g., start/stop, configuration adjustments).
  - **Control Message Formats**: Implicitly structured as **kinematic trajectory profiles** (position/velocity/acceleration over time) with no residual swinging motion at termination, as specified by the **Trajectory Generator Service**.
  - **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**: Not explicitly described, but the **Continuous Validation Service** cross-validates executed trajectories against simulations, implying a feedback loop for command accuracy. No authentication protocols (e.g., API keys, TLS) are mentioned for command transmission.
  - **Feedback Systems**: The **Logging Application** provides real-time state feedback to the Digital Twin, while the **Continuous Validation Service** monitors deviations between simulated and executed trajectories, alerting operators via the **Dashboard** if thresholds are breached.
  - **Conflict Resolution**: Not explicitly addressed, though the **Control Application** likely prioritizes commands based on timestamp or service origin (e.g., operator overrides vs. automated trajectories).
  - **Command Execution Confirmation**: Implicitly handled via the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares executed trajectories to simulations and triggers alerts for discrepancies.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure relies on a **service-oriented architecture** with the following specifics:
  - **Communication Protocols**:
    - **MQTT** for inter-service signaling (e.g., between **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, and **Continuous Validation Service**).
    - **TimescaleDB-native protocols** (likely **PostgreSQL/HTTP-based**) for structured data storage/retrieval.
    - **Python-based serial/TCP protocols** for **Physical Crane Interface** ↔ **Physical Crane** communication.
  - **Network Topology/Architecture**: Centralized with a **star topology** around the **MQTT Broker** and **TimescaleDB**, where services subscribe/publish to topics and query the database. No distributed or mesh topology is implied.
  - **Bandwidth/Latency Requirements**:
    - **Low-latency** for real-time control (e.g., trajectory execution) and validation (sub-100ms likely required for crane stability).
    - **Moderate bandwidth** for logging (continuous state updates to **TimescaleDB**).
  - **Reliability/Redundancy Mechanisms**: Not explicitly described, though the **Continuous Validation Service** suggests fault tolerance via trajectory comparison and operator alerts. No hardware/software redundancy (e.g., failover nodes) is mentioned.
  - **Security Protocols**: Not specified, but **TimescaleDB** (a PostgreSQL extension) typically supports **TLS/SSL** for data-in-transit security. MQTT may use **username/password or client certificates** for broker access, though not confirmed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is **on-premise** (co-located with the **Physical Crane**) with the following technical details:
  - **Hosting Platform**: Local servers or workstations running the **Digital Twin containers** (Python applications, **TimescaleDB**, **Grafana**, and **MQTT Broker**). No cloud or edge deployment is mentioned.
  - **Computational Requirements**:
    - **Trajectory Generator Service**: Requires kinematic modeling capabilities (likely **Python + numerical libraries** like NumPy/SciPy).
    - **Simulation Service**: Demands computational resources for real-time physics-based simulations of crane dynamics.
    - **Continuous Validation Service**: Moderate CPU for trajectory comparison metrics (distance calculations).
  - **Storage Requirements**: **TimescaleDB** (a time-series database) stores historical crane state data, with no explicit capacity limits provided. Logs are continuously appended.
  - **Scalability Mechanisms**: Not addressed; the system appears **monolithic** with no horizontal scaling (e.g., Kubernetes, load balancers) or microservice decomposition.
  - **Deployment Models**: **Single-node deployment** for all containers, with no container orchestration (e.g., Docker Swarm, Kubernetes) mentioned. Services are likely deployed as standalone Python processes or lightweight virtual environments.
  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: No explicit mechanisms (e.g., backups, high availability clusters) are described. Operator alerts via the **Dashboard** serve as a reactive reliability measure.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) employ **ad-hoc development approaches** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. Key methodologies include:
  - **Containerization/virtualization** for easy dissemination and deployment (e.g., virtual machines with pre-recorded data in GreenhouseDT, containerization in the gantry crane exemplar).
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** is explicitly mentioned for the **Air Quality Management** exemplar, leveraging **AutomationML** for digital twin representation and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation.
  - **Behavioral adaptation** is a core feature in **GreenhouseDT**, where both the physical system (greenhouse) and its digital twin dynamically adjust their behavior, though specific lifecycle transition mechanisms (e.g., CI/CD pipelines, governance models) are not detailed.
  - **Version control** and **evolution management** are implied via GitHub hosting (e.g., [GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT), [Air Quality Management](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)), but no explicit strategies (e.g., branching models, release cycles) are described.
  - **Quality assurance** and **testing** are not explicitly documented, though the use of **pre-recorded data** in GreenhouseDT’s VMs suggests simulated validation environments. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar includes **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** as validation applications, but no formal QA methodologies (e.g., unit testing, integration testing) are specified.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides **three core decision-support applications**:
  - **Runtime visualization**: Real-time monitoring of indoor air quality metrics (e.g., sensor data from a **Raspberry Pi**).
  - **Simulation**: Dynamic modeling of air quality dynamics under varying conditions (e.g., virus spread prevention scenarios).
  - **Prediction**: Forecasting future air quality states using data-driven analytics, enabled by **Microsoft Azure tools**.
  The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar focuses on **self-adaptive behavior**, implying **closed-loop decision-making** where insights from the digital twin (e.g., environmental conditions) trigger adjustments in the physical greenhouse (e.g., ventilation, lighting). However, specific **analytics capabilities** (e.g., ML models, rule-based engines) or **user interaction methods** (e.g., dashboards, APIs) are not detailed. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are also not specified. The **gantry crane** exemplar lacks explicit insights/decision-making descriptions in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar adheres to:
  - **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, ensuring **interoperability** with industrial automation standards.
  - **Microsoft Azure tools**, which may imply compliance with **cloud-native standards** (e.g., Azure IoT, Azure Digital Twins service), though exact certifications are not listed.
  The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses:
  - **InfluxDB** (a time-series database standard for IoT/OT systems) and **ActiveMQ** (a JMS-compliant message broker), suggesting alignment with **data interchange standards** (e.g., MQTT, AMQP).
  - No explicit **certification requirements** or **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO 27001, IEC 62443) are mentioned.
  The **gantry crane** exemplar does not reference standardization in the provided documents. Overall, **interoperability** is achieved via **open-source technologies** (e.g., Raspberry Pi, containerization) and **domain-specific standards** (AutomationML), but no formal **industry certifications** or **governance frameworks** are documented.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)





















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
